one_line_takeaway: A single fine-tuned LLaVA-7B model, trained on a new 100K-image multi-LMM-annotated dataset (FakeClue), detects synthetic/deepfake images and explains their artifacts in natural language -- matching or beating expert classifiers without any auxiliary detector.
key_finding: Framing detection as explanatory VQA (predicting artifact descriptions, not just a 'Real'/'Fake' token) substantially outperforms binary-label tuning and yields a single model that matches or beats dedicated expert classifiers while also being interpretable. On FakeClue, FakeVLM reaches 98.6 Acc / 98.1 F1, and on the out-of-distribution LOKI benchmark 84.3 Acc / 83.7 F1 -- exceeding human performance on LOKI (80.1 Acc) and the strongest expert baseline NPR (77.4 Acc / 80.0 F1). The authors report an average +7.7% Acc and +3.6% F1 over top LMM/expert baselines across FakeClue and LOKI. The ablation confirms the mechanism: LLaVA + explanatory text (84.3 Acc / 80.1 F1 on LOKI) beats LLaVA + linear head (81.6 Acc / 77.8 F1).
core_contribution: The paper introduces FakeVLM, a specialized large multimodal model (built on LLaVA-v1.5-7B) that both classifies images as real vs. synthetic AND generates natural-language explanations of the visual artifacts driving the decision, unifying detection and interpretability without an external expert classifier. Its second contribution is FakeClue, a >100K-image dataset spanning seven categories (animal, human, object, scenery, satellite, document, face manipulation) annotated with fine-grained artifact clues in natural language via a multi-LMM aggregation pipeline. The authors explicitly position FakeVLM against prior explainable-detection work (FakeShield, ForgeryGPT, SIDA, DD-VQA, FFAA, X2-DFD), stressing that their focus is artifacts from DIRECT image synthesis (structural/distortion/physical artifacts) rather than the transitional/edge artifacts of tampered/forged images those cited works target, and that FakeVLM needs no auxiliary expert model to reach expert-level classification.
threat_model: Defensive. The paper addresses the societal threat of AI-generated (AIGC/diffusion/Flux) synthetic imagery being misused for fake news, disinformation, and forged-facial scams, eroding trust in visual media. FakeVLM is a detection/forensics tool that flags synthetic images and explains why, aiming to improve transparency and trustworthiness of automated authenticity judgments. It is not an attack; it does not model an adaptive adversary attempting to evade the detector (robustness is only tested against benign distortions like JPEG/noise/resize, not adversarial perturbations).
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) FakeClue annotations come mostly from multi-LMM aggregation, so biases of the annotator models and under-capture of fine-grained artifacts may persist; they suggest adding human-expert validation. (2) FakeVLM has diminished sensitivity to high-fidelity synthetic images with imperceptible artifacts, needing methods that analyze latent statistical irregularities beyond visible artifact cues. Reviewer-noted additional gaps: robustness is evaluated only against benign distortions (JPEG, resize, Gaussian noise, flip/rotate/sharpen/contrast/blur), not adversarial/evasion attacks, so resilience against an adaptive attacker is untested; the 84.3 headline Acc on LOKI leaves substantial error room on OOD data; and human-portrait detection remains the weakest category due to fast-advancing face generators. The model is 7B and full-fine-tuned, implying non-trivial compute.

datasets_used: FakeClue (this paper's own, >100K images, 7 categories, 5,000-sample test set; built from ~80K images drawn from GenImage, FF++, Chameleon plus self-synthesized satellite/document data)
LOKI (ICLR 2025 OOD evaluation benchmark, human-annotated artifacts)
FF++ (FaceForensics++, C23 versions: DeepFakes, Face2Face, FaceSwap, NeuralTextures)
DD-VQA (ECCV 2024 face-domain artifact VQA dataset built on FF++)
quantitative_results: FakeClue detection: FakeVLM 98.6 Acc / 98.1 F1; explanation 58.0 ROUGE_L / 87.7 CSS
LOKI (OOD): FakeVLM 84.3 Acc / 83.7 F1 / 20.1 ROUGE_L / 58.2 CSS; beats human 80.1 Acc and expert NPR 77.4 Acc / 80.0 F1 and AIDE 65.6 Acc / 80.2 F1
Reported average gains: +7.7% Acc, +3.6% F1 over top LMM/expert baselines across FakeClue and LOKI
DMimage generalization: FakeVLM 94.0 overall Acc / 94.3 F1, vs SIDA 91.8 / 92.4 and AntifakePrompt 90.6 / 91.2
DD-VQA: FakeVLM 93.2 Acc / 93.1 F1 / 70.4 ROUGE_L / 86.6 CSS, vs best baseline Common-DF-TI 87.5 / 90.1 / 60.9 (authors cite +5.7% Acc, +3% F1, +9.5% ROUGE_L)
FF++ AUC(%): FakeVLM average 96.3 (FF-DF 97.2, FF-F2F 96.0, FF-FS 96.8, FF-NT 95.0), competitive with Face X-ray 95.9 and SRM 95.8
Ablation on LOKI: LLaVA+Explanatory Text 84.3 Acc / 80.1 F1 vs LLaVA+Linear Head 81.6 Acc / 77.8 F1
Robustness on FakeClue: origin 98.6 Acc; degrades gracefully under Blur3 (89.5), Rotate15 (90.9), JPEG70 (91.2), Gaussian10 (92.1)
baselines_compared: General LMMs: GPT-4o, Qwen2-VL-7B/72B, InternVL2-8B/40B, Deepseek-VL2/-small, LLaVA
Synthetic-detection expert models: CNNSpot, FreqNet, Fatformer, UnivFD, AIDE (trained on FakeClue), NPR (trained on FakeClue)
DMimage-set methods: Gram-Net, Fusing, LNP, AntifakePrompt, SIDA
Deepfake/DD-VQA baseline: Common-DF (Common-DF-T, Common-DF-I, Common-DF-TI variants)
FF++ deepfake experts: FWA, Face X-ray, SRM, CDFA
